response: 263 is prime. It has no divisors other than 1 and itself. (Quick check: not divisible by 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, or 13, which are all primes ≤ √263 ≈ 16.2.)
reasoning_content: We need to check if 263 is prime. Let's test divisibility by small primes: 2: no, 3: sum digits 2+6+3=11, not divisible by 3; 5: doesn't end in 0 or 5; 7: 7*37=259, remainder 4, so not; 11: 11*23=253, remainder 10; 13: 13*20=260, remainder 3; 17: 17*15=255, remainder 8; 19: 19*13=247, remainder 16; 23: 23*11=253, remainder 10; 29: 29*9=261, remainder 2; 31: 31*8=248, remainder 15; 37: 37*7=259, remainder 4; 41: 41*6=246, remainder 17; 43: 43*6=258, remainder 5; 47: 47*5=235, remainder 28; 53: 53*4=212, remainder 51; 59: 59*4=236, remainder 27; 61: 61*4=244, remainder 19; 67: 67*3=201, remainder 62; 71: 71*3=213, remainder 50; 73: 73*3=219, remainder 44; 79: 79*3=237, remainder 26; 83: 83*3=249, remainder 14; 89: 89*2=178, remainder 85; 97: 97*2=194, remainder 69; 101: 101*2=202, remainder 61; 103: 103*2=206, remainder 57; 107: 107*2=214, remainder 49; 109: 109*2=218, remainder 45; 113: 113*2=226, remainder 37; 127: 127*2=254, remainder 9; 131: 131*2=262, remainder 1; so not divisible by any prime up to sqrt(263) ~ 16.2? Actually sqrt(263) is about 16.2, but we've tested up to 13, 17, etc. Wait, sqrt(263) ≈ 16.22, so we only need to test primes up to 16: 2,3,5,7,11,13. That's enough. We tested 2,3,5,7,11,13 none divide. So 263 is prime. Quick sanity: yes, it's prime.
